elimination_hints.hint: Prejudice is pre-JUDGMENT — formed WITHOUT adequate knowledge. An assessment based on thorough knowledge would be an informed opinion, not prejudice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing prejudice (prejudgment without knowledge) with informed evaluation

elimination_hints.hint: Positive feelings toward all groups equally would be the ABSENCE of prejudice. Prejudice involves NEGATIVE attitudes toward specific groups.
elimination_hints.misconception: Describing the opposite of prejudice

elimination_hints.hint: Cultural preference becomes prejudice when it involves NEGATIVE attitudes toward OTHER groups. Prejudice specifically involves hostility or discrimination toward outgroups.
elimination_hints.misconception: Minimizing prejudice as harmless cultural preference

question_text: A stereotype is:
explanation: A stereotype is a generalized, simplified, and often inaccurate belief about the characteristics of a group — applied to ALL members of that group regardless of individual differences. Stereotypes represent the COGNITIVE component of prejudice. Examples: 'All women are emotional', 'All engineers are introverted', 'All elderly people are technologically incompetent.' Stereotypes simplify information processing but lead to biased judgments because they ignore individual variation within groups.
subject: psychology

options.text: A generalized, often oversimplified belief about the characteristics of a group applied to all its members
An accurate description of every member of a group based on research
A negative action taken against a person because of their group membership
An emotional response of fear toward unfamiliar groups

elimination_hints.hint: Stereotypes are OVERSIMPLIFIED generalizations, not accurate descriptions. They ignore individual variation within groups.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating stereotypes as factual group descriptions

elimination_hints.hint: Negative actions based on group membership describe DISCRIMINATION (the behavioural component). Stereotypes are BELIEFS (the cognitive component).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing stereotypes (beliefs) with discrimination (actions)

elimination_hints.hint: Emotional responses of fear or hostility are part of PREJUDICE (the affective component). Stereotypes are the COGNITIVE component — beliefs, not emotions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing stereotypes (cognitive) with prejudice (affective)

question_text: Discrimination differs from prejudice in that discrimination involves:
explanation: Discrimination is the BEHAVIOURAL component of prejudice — it involves actual ACTIONS (negative or differential treatment) toward individuals based on their group membership. While prejudice is an ATTITUDE (internal evaluation), discrimination is BEHAVIOUR (external action). A person can be prejudiced without discriminating (holding negative attitudes but not acting on them) or can discriminate without being personally prejudiced (following discriminatory institutional policies). Discrimination includes: exclusion, harassment, unequal treatment, and denial of opportunities.
subject: psychology

options.text: Holding negative beliefs about a group (cognitive)
Feeling hostility toward a group (emotional)
Actual negative or differential ACTIONS toward individuals based on their group membership (behavioural)
Having no opinion about any group

elimination_hints.hint: Negative beliefs are STEREOTYPES (cognitive component of prejudice). Discrimination is the BEHAVIOURAL component — actual actions, not beliefs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing discrimination (behaviour) with stereotypes (beliefs)

elimination_hints.hint: Emotional hostility is PREJUDICE (affective component). Discrimination is about DOING something negative, not just FEELING negatively.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing discrimination (action) with prejudice (feeling)

elimination_hints.hint: Having no opinion is indifference, not discrimination. Discrimination involves ACTIVE negative treatment based on group membership.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the absence of opinion with the presence of discriminatory action

question_text: The 'contact hypothesis' (Allport, 1954) suggests that prejudice can be reduced by:
explanation: Allport proposed that intergroup prejudice can be reduced through direct contact between groups, but ONLY under specific conditions: (1) Equal status — both groups have equal standing in the contact situation; (2) Common goals — groups work toward shared objectives; (3) Intergroup cooperation — groups cooperate rather than compete; (4) Institutional support — authority figures or laws support the contact. Without these conditions, contact can actually INCREASE prejudice. Successful contact reduces prejudice by challenging stereotypes through personal experience with outgroup members.
subject: psychology

options.text: Completely separating groups so they never interact
Direct contact between groups under conditions of equal status, common goals, cooperation, and institutional support
Any contact between groups, regardless of conditions
Only through legal punishment of prejudiced behaviour

elimination_hints.hint: Separation INCREASES prejudice by preventing opportunities to challenge stereotypes. Contact (under right conditions) is the solution, not separation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Proposing the opposite of the contact hypothesis

elimination_hints.hint: Allport specifically emphasized that contact must occur under SPECIFIC CONDITIONS. Without equal status, cooperation, and support, contact can worsen prejudice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Oversimplifying the contact hypothesis by ignoring its necessary conditions

elimination_hints.hint: Legal punishment addresses discrimination (behaviour) but not necessarily prejudice (attitude). The contact hypothesis targets attitude change through positive intergroup experiences.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing behavioural compliance with attitudinal change

question_text: Sherif's Robbers Cave experiment (1954) demonstrated that intergroup conflict could be reduced by:
explanation: In the Robbers Cave experiment, Sherif created conflict between two groups of boys at summer camp, then found that intergroup hostility was reduced by introducing SUPERORDINATE GOALS — objectives that required BOTH groups to cooperate because neither could achieve them alone (e.g., fixing the water supply, pooling money for a movie). Competition increased hostility; mere contact without cooperation did not help. Only shared goals requiring interdependent cooperation reduced prejudice — because the groups had to see each other as allies, not enemies.
subject: psychology

options.text: Increasing competition between the groups
Separating the groups permanently
Having one group dominate and control the other
Introducing superordinate goals — shared objectives that require cooperation between both groups

elimination_hints.hint: Competition actually CREATED the conflict in the experiment. Sherif showed that competition increases intergroup hostility, not reduces it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Proposing the cause of conflict as its solution

elimination_hints.hint: Separation would prevent conflict but not reduce prejudice. The experiment showed that COOPERATIVE CONTACT, not separation, reduced hostility.
elimination_hints.misconception: Proposing avoidance rather than cooperative engagement

elimination_hints.hint: Domination would create resentment and deepen conflict. Prejudice reduction required EQUAL, COOPERATIVE relationships.
elimination_hints.misconception: Proposing power imbalance as a solution when equality was key

question_text: Social identity theory (Tajfel) explains prejudice as arising from:
explanation: Tajfel's Social Identity Theory (1979) proposes that people derive part of their self-esteem from their GROUP memberships (ingroups). To maintain positive self-esteem, people favour their ingroup over outgroups — this ingroup favouritism leads to prejudice against outgroups. The process involves: (1) Social categorization — classifying people into groups; (2) Social identification — adopting the identity of the ingroup; (3) Social comparison — comparing ingroup favourably against outgroups. Prejudice is thus a consequence of the normal need for positive social identity.
subject: psychology

options.text: The need for positive self-esteem through ingroup favouritism and outgroup derogation
Individual personality defects unique to prejudiced people
Genetic predisposition to dislike all outgroup members
Conscious, rational analysis of group differences

elimination_hints.hint: Social identity theory views prejudice as a NORMAL group process, not a personality defect. Everyone engages in ingroup favouritism to some degree.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating prejudice as individual pathology rather than a group-level process

elimination_hints.hint: Prejudice is a SOCIAL/PSYCHOLOGICAL phenomenon, not genetic. Social identity theory explains it through group processes and self-esteem needs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing a social process to genetic causation

elimination_hints.hint: Prejudice from social identity is largely AUTOMATIC and driven by identity needs, not by rational analysis of actual group differences.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating an automatic identity-based process as rational analysis

question_text: A teacher who meets a student wearing expensive clothes unconsciously assumes the student is also intelligent, well-behaved, and from a good family. This illustrates:
explanation: This illustrates the halo effect — one positive characteristic (wearing expensive clothes/appearing wealthy) creates an overall positive impression that extends to unrelated characteristics (intelligence, behaviour, family background). The teacher is not consciously deciding this — it is an automatic cognitive bias. The halo effect is particularly problematic in educational settings because it can lead to biased expectations and self-fulfilling prophecies (Pygmalion effect).
subject: psychology

options.text: Fundamental Attribution Error
Halo effect
Self-serving bias
Cognitive dissonance

elimination_hints.hint: The FAE involves attributing behaviour to personality rather than situation. The scenario describes one TRAIT influencing judgments of ALL traits — which is the halo effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing halo effect (trait generalization) with FAE (situational discounting)

elimination_hints.hint: Self-serving bias involves taking credit for success and blaming failure on external factors. The teacher is making judgments about a STUDENT, not about themselves.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying a self-focused bias to an other-focused judgment

elimination_hints.hint: Cognitive dissonance involves conflicting cognitions. The teacher has no conflict — they are simply overgeneralizing from one positive trait.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing impression bias with cognitive conflict

question_text: A student who scores well on an exam says: 'I succeeded because I studied hard and I'm smart.' The same student, after failing another exam, says: 'That exam was unfair and the teacher didn't teach the material properly.' This pattern demonstrates:
explanation: This is a textbook self-serving bias — attributing success to INTERNAL factors (studied hard, smart = ability and effort) and failure to EXTERNAL factors (unfair exam, poor teaching = situation). This asymmetric attribution pattern protects self-esteem by allowing the person to take credit for positive outcomes while deflecting blame for negative ones. It is one of the most common and robust attribution biases.
subject: psychology

options.text: Fundamental Attribution Error
Actor-observer bias
Self-serving bias
Halo effect

elimination_hints.hint: The FAE applies to explaining OTHERS' behaviour. This student is explaining their OWN behaviour — taking credit for success, blaming external factors for failure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying FAE (about others) to self-attributions

elimination_hints.hint: Actor-observer bias is about DIFFERENT perspectives (actor vs observer). This scenario involves the SAME person making different attributions for different OUTCOMES (success vs failure).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing outcome-based asymmetry with perspective-based asymmetry

elimination_hints.hint: The halo effect involves one trait influencing overall impression. This scenario is about CAUSAL ATTRIBUTION for success and failure, not impression formation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing attribution bias with impression formation bias

question_text: Which of the following is a source of prejudice?
explanation: Social learning (observational learning from parents, peers, media) is a major source of prejudice. Children learn prejudiced attitudes by observing and imitating prejudiced models — hearing parents make negative comments about groups, watching media portrayals that stereotype groups, or observing peers exclude certain individuals. Other sources include: social identity (ingroup favouritism), realistic conflict (competition for resources), scapegoating (blaming outgroups for problems), and authoritarian personality.
subject: psychology

options.text: Having extensive personal contact with diverse groups under equal conditions
Learning prejudiced attitudes from parents, peers, and media through observation
Receiving higher education that promotes critical thinking
Practicing empathy and perspective-taking regularly

elimination_hints.hint: Equal-status contact with diverse groups REDUCES prejudice (contact hypothesis). It is a prejudice REDUCER, not a source.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a prejudice-reducing factor with a prejudice source

elimination_hints.hint: Education that promotes critical thinking typically REDUCES prejudice by encouraging questioning of stereotypes. It is a reducer, not a source.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing an anti-prejudice factor with a pro-prejudice factor

elimination_hints.hint: Empathy and perspective-taking REDUCE prejudice by helping people understand outgroup experiences. They are reducers, not sources.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing prejudice-reducing skills with prejudice sources

question_text: The recency effect in impression formation means:
explanation: The recency effect is the tendency for the most RECENTLY presented information to have a stronger impact on the overall impression. It is the opposite of the primacy effect. The recency effect is more likely to occur when: there is a long delay between early and late information (giving time for early impressions to fade), the person is warned to avoid first-impression bias, or when the person is fatigued and not processing early information carefully. In most situations, the primacy effect is stronger than the recency effect.
subject: psychology

options.text: First information always dominates the impression
One positive trait makes the entire impression positive
People always form accurate impressions over time
The most recently presented information has a stronger impact on the overall impression

elimination_hints.hint: First information dominating is the PRIMACY effect — the opposite of the recency effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing recency (last info dominates) with primacy (first info dominates)

elimination_hints.hint: One trait influencing all judgments is the HALO effect, not the recency effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing recency (information order) with halo (trait generalization)

elimination_hints.hint: The recency effect is a BIAS — it shows impressions are influenced by information order, not accuracy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming accuracy when the concept describes systematic bias

question_text:
Assertion (A): Stereotypes are resistant to change even when contradictory evidence is encountered.
Reason (R): People tend to explain away stereotype-inconsistent information as 'exceptions to the rule' rather than revising their stereotypical beliefs.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Stereotypes persist because of cognitive processes that protect them: (1) Confirmation bias — noticing stereotype-consistent behaviour and ignoring inconsistent behaviour; (2) Subtyping — treating contradictory examples as exceptions ('they're not like the others'); (3) Illusory correlation — overestimating the relationship between group membership and negative behaviour. R describes subtyping — the most common way stereotypes resist change.
subject: psychology

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A — the 'exception' strategy IS one of the main mechanisms by which stereotypes resist change despite contradictory evidence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting the subtyping mechanism with stereotype persistence

elimination_hints.hint: R is well-documented in social cognition research. Subtyping is an established mechanism of stereotype maintenance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the established cognitive mechanism

elimination_hints.hint: A is well-established. Stereotype persistence despite contradictory evidence is one of the most replicated findings in prejudice research.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that stereotypes are highly resistant to contradictory evidence

question_text:
Assertion (A): The Fundamental Attribution Error is weaker in collectivist cultures like India compared to individualist cultures like the USA.
Reason (R): Collectivist cultures emphasize the role of context, relationships, and situational factors in behaviour, making people more attentive to external causes.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Cross-cultural research (Miller, 1984; Morris & Peng, 1994) shows that the FAE is moderated by cultural orientation. In collectivist cultures (India, Japan, China), people give more weight to situational/contextual factors when explaining behaviour — because their cultural framework emphasizes interdependence, context, and social harmony. In individualist cultures (USA, UK), the focus on personal autonomy makes dispositional attributions more automatic.
subject: psychology

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A — the collectivist emphasis on context IS the mechanism that reduces the dispositional bias (FAE).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting cultural values with attribution patterns

elimination_hints.hint: R is supported by cross-cultural research. Collectivist cultures' emphasis on context and interdependence is well-documented.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the established cultural explanation

elimination_hints.hint: A is supported by multiple studies (Miller, 1984; Morris & Peng, 1994). The cultural moderation of FAE is well-established.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the cross-cultural evidence on attribution patterns

question_text:
Assertion (A): Education and awareness programs can help reduce prejudice.
Reason (R): Education promotes critical thinking that helps people question stereotypes, understand outgroup perspectives, and recognize their own biases.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Education reduces prejudice through multiple mechanisms: (1) Critical thinking — questioning the validity of stereotypes; (2) Perspective-taking — understanding experiences of outgroup members; (3) Metacognition — becoming aware of one's own biases; (4) Factual knowledge — correcting misinformation about outgroups; (5) Exposure — encountering diverse viewpoints. The NCERT textbook identifies education as one of the key strategies for prejudice reduction.
subject: psychology

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains HOW education reduces prejudice — through critical thinking, perspective-taking, and bias awareness.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting the educational mechanisms with prejudice reduction outcomes

elimination_hints.hint: R accurately describes established mechanisms by which education combats prejudice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the mechanisms of educational prejudice reduction

elimination_hints.hint: A is well-supported. Education is recognized by psychologists, the NCERT textbook, and UNESCO as an effective prejudice reduction strategy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying education's role in reducing prejudice

question_text: Stereotypes are always accurate descriptions of group characteristics.
explanation: FALSE. Stereotypes are OVERSIMPLIFIED generalizations that exaggerate group differences and ignore individual variation within groups. While some stereotypes may contain a 'kernel of truth' (a statistical tendency), they are applied rigidly to ALL group members regardless of individual characteristics. Many stereotypes are completely inaccurate, based on media portrayals, historical prejudice, or limited contact. The defining problem with stereotypes is that they deny individuality.
subject: psychology

False

question_text: The Fundamental Attribution Error applies equally when we explain our own behaviour and when we explain others' behaviour.

question_text: The cognitive component of prejudice — generalized beliefs applied to all members of a group — is called a _____.
explanation: A stereotype is the cognitive component of prejudice — it consists of generalized, usually oversimplified beliefs about the characteristics of a group applied to all its members. Stereotypes provide the 'knowledge base' for prejudice: the affective component (negative feelings) and behavioural component (discrimination) are built upon these cognitive generalizations. Stereotypes can be positive or negative, but both are problematic because they deny individuality.

question_text: The tendency to overestimate dispositional factors and underestimate situational factors when explaining others' behaviour is called the _____.
explanation: The Fundamental Attribution Error (FAE), also called correspondence bias, was named by Lee Ross (1977). It is 'fundamental' because it is so pervasive and widespread in social perception. When we see someone behave in a certain way, we automatically assume it reflects their CHARACTER (disposition) rather than their CIRCUMSTANCES (situation). For example, seeing a server being rude, we think 'rude person' rather than 'might be having a terrible day.'

question_text:
Match the attribution bias in Column I with its description in Column II:

Column I:
A. Fundamental Attribution Error

1. Actors explain own behaviour situationally; observers explain it dispositionally
2. Believing people get what they deserve — victims must have done something wrong
3. Taking credit for successes (internal) and blaming failures on external factors
4. Overestimating personality and underestimating situation when explaining others' behaviour
explanation: FAE (A) = overestimating disposition for others (4). Self-serving (B) = internal for success, external for failure (3). Actor-observer (C) = actors use situation, observers use disposition (1). Just-world (D) = believing victims deserve outcomes (2).
subject: psychology

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns self-serving (3) to FAE. The FAE is about others' behaviour; self-serving is about own success/failure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing an other-focused bias with a self-focused bias

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns actor-observer (1) to FAE. While related, the FAE specifically describes the dispositional bias for others; actor-observer describes the broader perspective asymmetry.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between the specific FAE and the broader actor-observer pattern

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns just-world (2) to FAE. The FAE is about dispositional overweighting; the just-world hypothesis is about believing outcomes are deserved.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing two distinct attribution phenomena

question_text: In a diverse school, Hindu and Muslim students initially show mutual distrust. The principal organizes a joint community service project where students from both groups must work together to build a playground for underprivileged children. Over time, the students develop friendships across groups and prejudice decreases. This outcome is best explained by:
explanation: This scenario demonstrates BOTH the contact hypothesis (Allport) and superordinate goals (Sherif). The conditions for effective prejudice reduction are met: (1) Equal status — all students are peers; (2) Common goal — building the playground; (3) Intergroup cooperation — they must work together; (4) Institutional support — the principal organized it. The superordinate goal (building the playground) requires interdependence — neither group can complete it alone. This forced cooperation creates positive experiences and cross-group friendships.
subject: psychology

options.text: Cognitive dissonance theory
The Fundamental Attribution Error
The contact hypothesis (Allport) and superordinate goals (Sherif) — cooperative contact under equal conditions reduces prejudice
The halo effect — one positive trait changes everything

elimination_hints.hint: Cognitive dissonance involves conflicting beliefs within one person. The scenario describes INTERGROUP conflict reduction through cooperation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying an intrapersonal theory to an intergroup situation

elimination_hints.hint: The FAE is about individual attribution bias. The scenario describes GROUP-LEVEL prejudice reduction through structured cooperative contact.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying an individual cognitive bias to a group prejudice scenario

elimination_hints.hint: The halo effect is about one trait influencing overall impression of a person. The scenario describes systematic, structured intergroup contact reducing prejudice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying an impression formation bias to a prejudice reduction intervention

question_text:
The diagram shows an attribution decision process as a flowchart:

Box 1: 'Person performs a behaviour' →
Box 2: 'Observer asks: WHY did they do that?' →
Branch A: 'INTERNAL ATTRIBUTION — The behaviour was caused by the person's personality, character, or disposition'
Branch B: 'EXTERNAL ATTRIBUTION — The behaviour was caused by the situation, circumstances, or environment'

According to the diagram, if a student sees a classmate sleeping in class and thinks 'She is lazy and doesn't care about studies,' the student is making:
image_alt: Flowchart: 'Person performs behaviour' → 'Observer asks WHY?' → Two branches: Branch A (Internal Attribution: personality, character, disposition) and Branch B (External Attribution: situation, circumstances, environment).
explanation: The student is making an INTERNAL (dispositional) attribution — explaining the classmate's sleeping as due to her personality ('lazy', 'doesn't care') rather than considering situational factors (she might be ill, working night shifts, or dealing with a family crisis). This is also an example of the Fundamental Attribution Error — the observing student overestimates dispositional factors without considering the situation.
subject: psychology

options.text: An internal (dispositional) attribution — explaining behaviour through the person's character traits
An external (situational) attribution — explaining behaviour through circumstances
No attribution at all — the observer has no opinion
A balanced attribution considering both personality and situation equally

elimination_hints.hint: External attribution would involve thinking about SITUATIONS: 'Maybe she is ill, or worked late, or has a family problem.' Calling someone 'lazy' is clearly attributing to personality.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a personality judgment (internal) with a situational explanation (external)

elimination_hints.hint: The student explicitly thought 'she is lazy and doesn't care' — this IS an attribution (a causal explanation for the behaviour).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing that a character judgment IS an attribution

elimination_hints.hint: The student did NOT consider situational factors at all. 'Lazy and doesn't care' is entirely dispositional — there is no balance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Claiming balance when the attribution is entirely one-sided

question_text: Scapegoating as a source of prejudice involves:
explanation: Scapegoating occurs when a group that is frustrated or experiencing problems blames a weaker, visible outgroup for their difficulties — even when that outgroup is not responsible. The outgroup becomes a 'scapegoat' — a target for displaced aggression and blame. This is based on the frustration-aggression hypothesis: frustration produces aggression, and when the true source of frustration is too powerful to attack, aggression is displaced onto a safer target (an outgroup). Historical examples include blaming immigrant communities for economic problems.
subject: psychology

options.text: Accurately identifying the true cause of a social problem
Working cooperatively with outgroups to solve shared problems
Providing equal opportunities for all groups
Blaming a weaker outgroup for problems they did not cause — displacing frustration onto a convenient target

elimination_hints.hint: Scapegoating is the OPPOSITE of accurate identification — it involves MISPLACING blame on an innocent outgroup.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing scapegoating (false blame) with accurate problem identification

elimination_hints.hint: Cooperating with outgroups is a SOLUTION to prejudice (superordinate goals), not the cause.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a prejudice solution with a prejudice source

elimination_hints.hint: Equal opportunities REDUCE prejudice. Scapegoating is a CAUSE of prejudice, not a solution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing prejudice reduction with prejudice causation

question_text:
Which of the following statements about prejudice is correct?

I. Prejudice has cognitive (stereotypes), affective (negative feelings), and behavioural (discrimination) components.
II. The contact hypothesis states that any contact between groups always reduces prejudice.
III. Social learning from parents and media is a major source of prejudice.
IV. Superordinate goals requiring intergroup cooperation can reduce prejudice.
explanation: Statements I, III, and IV are correct. I: Prejudice has three established components (cognitive, affective, behavioural). III: Social learning is a well-documented prejudice source. IV: Sherif's Robbers Cave demonstrated superordinate goals reduce prejudice. Statement II is FALSE — the contact hypothesis specifies CONDITIONS (equal status, common goals, cooperation, institutional support). Without these conditions, contact can actually INCREASE prejudice.
subject: psychology

options.text: I and II only
All four are correct
I, III, and IV only
II and IV only

elimination_hints.hint: Statement II is false — Allport specifically stated that conditions (equal status, cooperation, etc.) must be met. Unqualified contact does not always reduce prejudice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Oversimplifying the contact hypothesis

elimination_hints.hint: Statement II is false. Without the required conditions, contact can actually worsen prejudice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not critically evaluating the contact hypothesis claim

elimination_hints.hint: Statement II is false (contact requires conditions) and this excludes I and III (both correct).
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting the oversimplified contact claim while rejecting established facts

question_text: The 'just-world hypothesis' suggests that people tend to believe:
explanation: The just-world hypothesis (Lerner, 1980) is the belief that the world is fair — people get what they deserve and deserve what they get. This belief leads to VICTIM-BLAMING: when bad things happen to people, observers assume the victim must have done something to cause or deserve it. Examples: believing a crime victim 'asked for it' or a poor person 'didn't work hard enough.' This bias protects the observer's sense of security ('if I behave well, bad things won't happen to me') but causes harm to victims.
subject: psychology

options.text: The world is fair — people get what they deserve, which can lead to blaming victims for their misfortune
The world is completely random and unfair — nothing anyone does matters
Only good things happen to good people, and this is scientifically proven
Justice systems are always perfect and never make mistakes

elimination_hints.hint: The just-world hypothesis is the OPPOSITE of believing the world is random. People WANT to believe in fairness and order.
elimination_hints.misconception: Describing the opposite of the just-world belief

elimination_hints.hint: The just-world hypothesis is a COGNITIVE BIAS — it is NOT scientifically true. Bad things happen to good people. The bias is about what people WANT to believe.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a cognitive bias as a scientific fact

elimination_hints.hint: The just-world hypothesis is about personal beliefs about cosmic fairness, not about evaluating justice systems.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a general fairness belief with an evaluation of legal systems

question_text: An 'internal attribution' for behaviour means explaining the behaviour as caused by:
explanation: An internal (dispositional) attribution explains behaviour as caused by factors WITHIN the person — their personality, character, attitudes, abilities, motivation, or effort. Examples: 'She succeeded because she is talented' (ability), 'He failed because he didn't try hard enough' (effort), 'She is always late because she is irresponsible' (personality). Internal attributions hold the PERSON responsible for their behaviour, as opposed to external attributions which point to situational factors.
subject: psychology

options.text: Situational factors, circumstances, or the environment
The person's personality, character, abilities, or effort — factors within the person
Luck, chance, or random events
Social pressure and group conformity

elimination_hints.hint: Situational factors are EXTERNAL (environmental) attributions. Internal attributions focus on the PERSON, not the situation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing internal (person-based) with external (situation-based) attributions

elimination_hints.hint: Luck and chance are EXTERNAL attributions — they point to factors outside the person's control.
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing luck/chance (external) in the internal category

elimination_hints.hint: Social pressure is an EXTERNAL (situational) factor. Internal attributions focus on the individual's characteristics, not social forces.
elimination_hints.misconception: Classifying external social pressures as internal factors

question_text: Asch's (1946) research on impression formation found that certain traits have a disproportionate influence on overall impressions. He called these:
explanation: Asch found that certain traits (like 'warm' vs 'cold') dramatically changed overall impressions even when all other traits remained the same. He called these 'central traits' because they are central to impression formation — they serve as organizing themes around which other information is interpreted. Peripheral traits (like 'polite' vs 'blunt') had much less impact. Note: Asch's 'central traits' in impression formation are different from Allport's 'central traits' in personality — same term, different context.
subject: psychology

options.text: Peripheral traits — minor characteristics with little impact
Cardinal traits — all-encompassing personality traits
Central traits — traits that have a disproportionate influence on overall impression formation
Secondary traits — context-specific preferences

elimination_hints.hint: Peripheral traits have LITTLE impact on impression. The question describes traits with DISPROPORTIONATELY LARGE impact — which Asch called central traits.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing high-impact traits (central) with low-impact traits (peripheral)

elimination_hints.hint: Cardinal traits are ALLPORT's concept about personality (all-encompassing traits). Asch's central traits are about IMPRESSION FORMATION — different context.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Allport's personality classification with Asch's impression formation research

elimination_hints.hint: Secondary traits are ALLPORT's concept about situation-specific preferences. Asch's research was about impression formation, not personality classification.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing Allport's personality categories with Asch's impression formation findings

question_text: Which of the following strategies can help reduce prejudice?
explanation: Multiple strategies can reduce prejudice: (1) Intergroup contact under Allport's conditions (equal status, cooperation, common goals, institutional support); (2) Superordinate goals requiring cooperation (Sherif); (3) Education and awareness — critical thinking, perspective-taking; (4) Empathy training — understanding outgroup experiences; (5) Legal measures — anti-discrimination laws; (6) Media representation — positive, accurate portrayals of diverse groups; (7) Jigsaw classroom (Aronson) — cooperative learning. Increasing competition would INCREASE prejudice, not reduce it.
subject: psychology

options.text: Increasing competition between groups
Completely separating groups to prevent any interaction
Promoting only one group's perspective in media
Cooperative intergroup contact, education, empathy training, and positive media representation

elimination_hints.hint: Competition INCREASES prejudice — Sherif's Robbers Cave showed that intergroup competition creates hostility. Cooperation, not competition, reduces prejudice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Proposing the cause of prejudice as its solution

elimination_hints.hint: Separation prevents the positive contact needed to challenge stereotypes. Contact (under right conditions), not separation, reduces prejudice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Proposing isolation when contact is the solution

elimination_hints.hint: Promoting only one perspective reinforces ethnocentrism and prejudice. Balanced, diverse media representation is what reduces prejudice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Proposing one-sided representation when diversity is needed

question_text: After losing his job, Arjun starts blaming immigrants for 'stealing jobs from locals,' even though his company downsized due to automation. Arjun is engaging in:
explanation: Arjun is engaging in scapegoating — blaming a visible, less powerful outgroup (immigrants) for problems they did not cause (his job loss was due to automation). This is consistent with the frustration-aggression hypothesis: Arjun is frustrated (job loss), but cannot attack the real cause (automation/technology is abstract), so he displaces his anger onto a convenient, visible target (immigrants). Scapegoating provides a simple, satisfying explanation for complex problems but unfairly targets innocent groups.
subject: psychology

options.text: Scapegoating — blaming a weaker outgroup for problems they did not cause
Accurate attribution — correctly identifying the cause of his job loss
Self-serving bias — protecting his self-esteem
Cognitive dissonance — resolving conflicting beliefs

elimination_hints.hint: The scenario explicitly states automation caused the job loss, NOT immigrants. Arjun's attribution is INACCURATE — that is what makes it scapegoating.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating scapegoating as accurate causal analysis

elimination_hints.hint: While self-esteem protection may play a role, the defining feature here is BLAMING AN OUTGROUP for problems caused by something else — which is specifically scapegoating.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using a general self-protective concept when a specific prejudice mechanism applies

elimination_hints.hint: There are no conflicting cognitions described. Arjun is simply misdirecting blame, not resolving internal inconsistency.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying dissonance theory when the scenario describes displaced blame

question_text: Prejudice that exists at the institutional level — built into laws, policies, or organizational practices — is called:
explanation: Institutional (or systemic) discrimination refers to prejudice embedded in the structures, policies, and practices of organizations and institutions — even when individual members may not personally hold prejudiced attitudes. Examples include discriminatory hiring practices, unequal access to education or healthcare, biased criminal justice policies, and unequal resource allocation. It operates through systems and structures rather than individual acts, making it harder to identify and address.
subject: psychology

options.text: Personal prejudice
Cognitive dissonance
Institutional or systemic discrimination
The halo effect

elimination_hints.hint: Personal prejudice is held by INDIVIDUALS. Institutional discrimination is embedded in SYSTEMS and structures — it can operate even without individually prejudiced members.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing individual-level prejudice with system-level discrimination

elimination_hints.hint: Cognitive dissonance is about conflicting beliefs within a person. Institutional discrimination is about structural inequalities in organizations and systems.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing an intrapersonal cognitive process with a systemic structural issue

elimination_hints.hint: The halo effect is an individual impression formation bias. Institutional discrimination operates at the organizational/systemic level.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing an individual cognitive bias with a systemic structural issue

question_text: A student evaluates the claim: 'Prejudice is entirely caused by ignorance — education alone can eliminate it completely.' Which evaluation is most accurate?
explanation: The claim is an oversimplification. While ignorance/lack of education IS one source, prejudice has MULTIPLE causes: social identity needs (ingroup favouritism), realistic conflict (competition for resources), social learning (from parents, peers, media), scapegoating (displaced frustration), and institutional structures. Education helps but cannot address all these causes alone. A comprehensive approach requires: education AND intergroup contact AND institutional reform AND media representation AND legal protections. No single strategy eliminates prejudice completely.
subject: psychology

options.text: Completely correct — education alone can eliminate all prejudice
Completely wrong — education has no effect on prejudice at all
Oversimplified — ignorance is ONE source but prejudice has multiple causes (social identity, competition, social learning, institutional structures); comprehensive solutions are needed
Cannot be evaluated — no research exists on the causes of prejudice

elimination_hints.hint: Prejudice persists even among educated people because it has causes beyond ignorance — social identity needs, competition, and institutional structures are not solved by education alone.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reducing a multi-causal phenomenon to a single cause

elimination_hints.hint: Education DOES reduce prejudice (through critical thinking, perspective-taking). The claim is not completely wrong — it is partially correct but oversimplified.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying any role for education in prejudice reduction

elimination_hints.hint: Prejudice is one of the MOST researched topics in social psychology. Extensive research on causes and reduction strategies exists.
elimination_hints.misconception: Claiming ignorance about a heavily researched topic

question_text: In Solomon Asch's impression formation research, when the trait 'warm' was replaced by 'cold' in a list of personality descriptions, the overall impression changed dramatically. This demonstrated:
explanation: Asch (1946) presented two groups with identical lists of traits (intelligent, skillful, industrious, determined, practical, cautious) except one group had 'warm' and the other had 'cold.' The 'warm' group formed significantly more positive overall impressions than the 'cold' group. This demonstrated that certain traits (warm/cold) are 'central traits' in impression formation — they disproportionately influence the overall evaluation. Other traits (polite/blunt) had much less impact and were called 'peripheral traits.'
subject: psychology

options.text: All traits contribute equally to impression formation
Certain 'central traits' like warm/cold have a disproportionately large influence on overall impressions
Physical appearance is more important than personality traits
Impressions cannot be formed from trait descriptions alone

elimination_hints.hint: Asch's finding was precisely that traits do NOT contribute equally. 'Warm' vs 'cold' caused dramatic changes while 'polite' vs 'blunt' had minimal effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Claiming equal trait contribution when the study demonstrated unequal influence

elimination_hints.hint: Asch's study used only verbal trait descriptions — no physical appearance information. The finding is about trait centrality, not appearance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Introducing physical appearance into a study that only used verbal descriptions

elimination_hints.hint: Asch's study demonstrated that participants DID form clear, differentiated impressions from trait descriptions alone — very effectively.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the effectiveness of trait-based impression formation

question_text: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between stereotypes, prejudice, and discrimination?
explanation: Stereotypes, prejudice, and discrimination represent the three components of intergroup bias: Stereotypes = COGNITIVE component (what we think/believe about a group); Prejudice = AFFECTIVE component (what we feel toward a group); Discrimination = BEHAVIOURAL component (how we act toward group members). They typically reinforce each other: stereotypes provide the 'justification,' prejudice provides the 'motivation,' and discrimination is the 'action.' However, they can exist independently — a person may hold stereotypes without discriminating.
subject: psychology

options.text: They are all identical concepts with no differences
Stereotypes are behaviours, prejudice is thinking, and discrimination is feeling
They exist completely independently and never influence each other
Stereotypes are cognitive (beliefs), prejudice is affective (feelings), and discrimination is behavioural (actions) — they are related but distinct

elimination_hints.hint: They are RELATED but DISTINCT — each represents a different component (cognitive, affective, behavioural). A person can hold stereotypes without discriminating.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating three distinct components as identical

elimination_hints.hint: This REVERSES the components. Stereotypes are COGNITIVE (beliefs), not behaviours. Discrimination is BEHAVIOURAL (actions), not feelings.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misassigning the components to the wrong categories

elimination_hints.hint: While they can exist independently, they typically REINFORCE each other. Stereotypes inform prejudice, which motivates discrimination.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the interconnection between the three components

question_text: The realistic conflict theory explains prejudice as arising from:
explanation: Realistic Conflict Theory (Sherif et al.) proposes that prejudice arises when groups compete for LIMITED, VALUED RESOURCES — jobs, territory, political power, economic opportunities. When groups perceive that another group threatens their access to resources, hostility and prejudice develop. Sherif's Robbers Cave experiment demonstrated this: competition for prizes between two groups of boys created strong intergroup hostility. The theory emphasizes that prejudice is often rooted in real (not imagined) conflicts of interest between groups.
subject: psychology

options.text: Competition between groups for limited, valued resources such as jobs, territory, or power
Genetic differences between groups that cause natural hostility
Individual personality disorders that make some people prejudiced
The absence of any real differences between groups

elimination_hints.hint: Realistic conflict theory emphasizes SOCIAL/ECONOMIC competition, not genetics. Prejudice is a social phenomenon arising from resource competition.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing a social process to biological/genetic causation

elimination_hints.hint: The theory explains prejudice as a GROUP-LEVEL phenomenon driven by intergroup competition, not individual personality pathology.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reducing a group-level theory to individual personality explanations

elimination_hints.hint: The theory proposes that REAL conflicts of interest exist — competition for genuine resources. It is 'realistic' conflict, not imagined.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misunderstanding 'realistic' to mean differences don't exist

question_text: Which of the following best evaluates the claim: 'The contact hypothesis is the complete solution to prejudice — just bring groups together and prejudice disappears'?
explanation: The claim oversimplifies the contact hypothesis. Allport specifically identified FOUR conditions necessary for contact to reduce prejudice: equal status, common goals, cooperation, and institutional support. Without these, contact can actually INCREASE hostility. Additionally, even optimal contact has limitations: it may improve attitudes toward specific outgroup members without changing attitudes toward the entire outgroup (decategorization problem). A comprehensive approach combines contact with education, legal reform, and structural change.
subject: psychology

options.text: Completely correct — any contact immediately eliminates prejudice
Oversimplified — contact works only under specific conditions (equal status, cooperation, common goals, support); without these, it can worsen prejudice
Completely wrong — contact never reduces prejudice under any conditions
Cannot be evaluated — no research has tested the contact hypothesis

elimination_hints.hint: Unconditional contact can WORSEN prejudice. Allport specified four conditions that MUST be met for contact to reduce prejudice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the necessary conditions for effective contact

elimination_hints.hint: Under the right conditions, contact DOES reduce prejudice — this is well-supported by meta-analyses (Pettigrew & Tropp, 2006). The hypothesis is valid but conditional.
elimination_hints.misconception: Rejecting the contact hypothesis entirely when conditional support exists

elimination_hints.hint: The contact hypothesis is one of the MOST tested ideas in social psychology, with hundreds of studies and major meta-analyses.
elimination_hints.misconception: Claiming no evidence for one of the most studied hypotheses in social psychology
